hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: You are given an array $$$a$$$. How to count the number of arrays $$$b$$$ that satisfy $$$f(b) = a$$$ and $$$ \forall 1 \le i \le n, b_i \le r_i $$$.?

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: You are given an array $$$a$$$. How to count the number of arrays $$$b$$$ that satisfy $$$f(f(b)) = a$$$ and $$$ \forall 1 \le i \le n, b_i \le r_i $$$.?

hints.title: Hint 3
hints.content: How many arrays $$$a$$$ satisfy there exists an array $$$b$$$ such that $$$f(f(b)) = a$$$?

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2222H - Counting Sort?
After connecting all arrays $$$ a $$$ to $$$ f(a) $$$ with directed edges, we obtain a directed pseudoforest (a collection of directed trees where each tree has a single cycle at its root). Specifically, the array $$$ a = [0, 0, 0, \dots, 0] $$$ forms an isolated node, while $$$ a = [1, 0, 0, \dots, 0] $$$ serves as the root for all other nodes. Here, $$$ g(a) $$$ corresponds to the depth of $$$ a $$$ in its tree plus one.
Directly characterizing $$$ g $$$ is quite challenging, so we resort to a more brute-force approach. We observe that after removing two layers of leaves, the number of remaining nodes in the tree becomes manageable. Specifically, we focus on counting the number of arrays $$$ a $$$ such that there exists a array $$$b$$$ that satisfies $$$ f(f(b)) = a $$$.
It turns out that this constraint is equivalent to $$$ \sum_{i=1}^n i \times a_i \le n $$$. Let $$$ P(n) $$$ denote the number of integer partitions of $$$ n $$$. Then, the number of such arrays $$$ a $$$ is $$$ \sum_{i=0}^n P(i) $$$. For $$$ n = 50 $$$, this count is at most $$$ 1.3 \times 10^6 $$$.
We first preprocess all such arrays $$$ a $$$ along with their depths. Then, for each $$$ a $$$, we compute the number of arrays $$$ b $$$ satisfying $$$ f(f(b)) = a $$$ and $$$ \forall 1 \le i \le n, b_i \le r_i $$$. Except for the case where $$$ a = [1, 0, 0, \dots, 0] $$$, we have $$$ g(b) = g(a) + 2 $$$. We accumulate these counts into the final answer and handle the special cases where $$$ g(*) \le 3 $$$ separately.
Next, we discuss how to compute these counts. First, for a given $$$ a $$$, we determine the number of arrays $$$ b $$$ such that $$$ f(b) = a $$$ and $$$ \forall 1 \le i \le n, b_i \le r_i $$$. This can be done by filling the array $$$ b $$$ from the largest value to the smallest, with the coefficients being products of several binomial coefficients.
We incorporate this process into a dynamic programming (DP) approach. Let $$$ dp_{i, a} $$$ denote the number of valid arrays $$$ b $$$ where we have already determined $$$ f(b)_i, f(b)_{i+1}, \dots, f(b)_n $$$, and $$$ f(f(b)) = a $$$. The DP can be updated in $$$ \mathcal{O}(n) $$$ time per state by enumerating the possible values of $$$ f(b)_i $$$. Thus, for a given $$$ a $$$, the total number of valid arrays $$$ b $$$ is $$$ dp_{1, a} $$$.
The overall time complexity is $$$ \mathcal{O}(n^2 \sum_{i=0}^n P(i)) $$$. In practice, the number of possible transitions is only about $$$ 2.5 \times 10^8 $$$, which is significantly smaller than this upper bound, making the solution feasible.
